scenario: A multi-department process: Sales, Finance, Legal departments each do work. Team wonders if to model as Pools (one per department) or Lanes (within one process).
question: Pool vs Lane modeling choice?

options.text: Pools and Lanes are identical BPMN constructs for organizing tasks â€” this claim does not fully apply. Pools and Lanes serve fundamentally different modeling purposes. A Pool represents a separate BPMN participant or process participant with an independent executable process definition. A Lane is a sub-division within a Pool used for visual responsibility assignment. Pools communicate via Message Flows across boundaries; Lanes share sequence flows within one Pool. Treating them as identical leads to architectural mistakes â€” using separate Pools when Lanes suffice creates unnecessary process fragmentation.

Pools represent separate executable processes (participants in a collaboration) that communicate via Message Flows. Using separate Pools for Sales, Finance, and Legal within the same company would create three independent process definitions requiring message choreography between them -- far more complex than necessary. For departments within one organization sharing a single business process, Lanes within one Pool are the idiomatic modeling choice, maintaining a single executable process with clear responsibility assignments per lane.

Different semantic levels: Pools represent separate participants or organizations each with their own executable process definition, communicating via Message Flows (dashed arrows) across pool boundaries -- never sequence flows. Lanes are sub-divisions within a single Pool, visually assigning tasks to specific roles or departments within one shared process. For Sales, Finance, and Legal within the same company running one coordinated process, Lanes in a single Pool is the typical modeling choice: one deployed process, lanes for responsibility assignment, sequence flows crossing lane boundaries naturally.

, your company + external customer + partner system) where each party has an independent process. Pools are also appropriate for documenting cross-organization interactions with black-box pools showing external systems. Dismissing Pools entirely overlooks their role in collaboration diagrams and inter-organizational process documentation.

a.text: Incorrect. Pool vs Lane semantics: Pool: Participant in a collaboration. Represents an organisation / customer / system. Own process: each pool has its own internal process (or is a "black box" pool with hidden internals). Communication between pools: Message Flow (dashed arrows), NOT sequence flows. Engine semantic: typically one process per pool -- separate deployments. Lane: Sub-division within a pool. Organisational responsibility: which role / department handles each task. Same process: lanes don't split the process; just visualise responsibility. Sequence flows cross lanes: tasks in different lanes connected by normal flows. In Camunda 8: lanes are typically visual; runtime behavior same as if no lanes. For the scenario (Sales + Finance + Legal in one process): Option A: Lanes (single pool): One process: "Customer Onboarding". Lanes: Sales, Finance, Legal. Tasks placed in respective lanes. Sequence flows naturally cross lanes. Single executable process. Option B: Pools (separate processes): Three separate processes: SalesProcess, FinanceProcess, LegalProcess. Each pool internally has its own flow. Collaboration: message flows between pools (Sales sends "ready for finance" message). Three executable processes: each deployed separately. When to choose Lanes: Single business workflow with multiple departments contributing. Tight coupling: one process, multiple actors. Easier modelling: single process, clear handoffs. Most common for typical enterprise workflows. When to choose Pools: Genuine cross-organisation: customer + your company + partner. Loose coupling: each pool has own lifecycle.
